app.name: Twinzy
app.subtitle: Upload a photo and get a fun style/vibe result based on visible traits.
app.description: A free, playful style/vibe game based on written visible traits. No face recognition, no identity matching, and your photo is never stored.
app.footerNote: Twinzy is a free game for entertainment only.

home.step2: We read visible traits like hair, eyes, and face shape.
home.step3: You get playful public style/vibe matches. That is all.
home.privacyNotice: We do not store your photo. It is only used to extract visible traits for this game.
home.metaTitle: Free AI style & vibe match game
home.metaDescription: Upload one photo and get playful public-figure style matches built from written visible traits. Free, consent-first, no accounts, no face recognition — and your photo is never stored.
home.whatIsBody1: Twinzy is a free browser game that looks at one photo you choose, writes down the visible style traits it can see — hair color and length, glasses, beard style, face shape, overall look — and then suggests public figures whose publicly known style has a similar overall vibe. It is a playful style-twin generator, not a lookalike finder and not an identification tool.
home.whatIsBody2: The whole experience is a single round: you pick a photo, confirm you are happy for it to be analyzed, and within moments you get a short, readable list of playful matches with a score, a reason, and the exact traits that drove each suggestion. There is no account, no profile, and nothing to install.
home.howBody1: When you press analyze, our server reads your photo once and turns what is visible into a short written description — then the photo is wiped from memory immediately, whether the round succeeds or fails. From that moment on, only the written description exists anywhere in the game.
home.howBody2: Those written traits are compared against publicly known style descriptions of public figures, scored for overall vibe similarity, and returned as ranked playful matches. The how-it-works page walks through every step in order, including exactly what the AI is allowed to do at each stage.
home.privacyValueBody1: Most photo apps store your image; Twinzy is built so it cannot. Your photo is processed only in memory, is never written to disk, never logged, and never used to train anything. Once its visible traits have been written down, the image is gone for good.
home.privacyValueBody2: There is no face recognition and no biometric template of any kind: matching happens purely over written text like "short curly hair" or "round glasses". The privacy page explains exactly what is processed in a round and what never leaves it.
home.limitsTitle: What the AI can and cannot do
home.limitsBody1: The AI only describes what is visible in a single photo, so lighting, angle, filters, and image quality directly change the result. Two photos of the same person can produce different matches — that is expected, because it reads style, not identity.
home.limitsBody2: It cannot recognize who you are, cannot judge beauty, personality, or character, and refuses to guess anything sensitive. When a photo is too unclear for a confident playful answer, it says so honestly instead of inventing one.
home.safetyBody1: Every AI answer is checked against strict rules before you see it: no identifying real people, no guesses about ethnicity, religion, health, or other sensitive traits, and no clinical or biometric language. Anything that breaks a rule is rejected or cleaned before display.
home.safetyBody2: These are not settings that can be switched off — they are enforced in code on every single round. The AI-safety page describes each principle and why it exists.
home.resultsBody1: Each match shows a rank, a style-similarity score, and a plain-language reason listing which written traits matched well, which were weak, and where there were clear mismatches. The score describes overall vibe overlap — never how much you "look like" someone.
home.resultsBody2: Treat every result as a playful conversation starter. It can be wrong, it changes with photo quality, and it never means you resemble, are related to, or share anything with the matched person beyond a written style description.

result.title: Your vibe matches
result.disclaimer: This is a playful style/vibe result based on written visible traits only. It is not face recognition, identity matching, or biometric comparison.

result.scoreExplanation: The percentage is a playful style/vibe fit estimate from written traits, not facial similarity.
result.uncertaintyExplanation: Less-certain traits are soft guesses where the photo did not give a clear signal.
result.mismatchExplanation: Watch-outs show traits that visibly differ from the suggested match.

about.metaDescription: Twinzy is a privacy-first entertainment project: one photo becomes a short written style description, the photo is wiped, and playful text-only matches are suggested. Learn who built it and why.
about.title: About Twinzy
about.intro: Twinzy is a small, privacy-first entertainment project with one job: turn a single photo into a playful, text-based style match — and forget the photo immediately.
about.storyBody1: Twinzy started with a simple observation: people love "who is your style twin" games, but almost every one of them quietly collects faces. We wanted the fun without the collection — a game that could be enjoyed for a minute and then leave no trace at all.
about.storyBody2: So the pipeline was designed backwards from a single promise: the photo may be read once, in memory, and never kept. Everything else — the text-only matching, the wipe-in-every-case handling, the refusal to identify anyone — follows from that starting point.
about.differentBody1: Twinzy never compares faces. The moment your photo is analyzed it becomes a short written description — "wavy shoulder-length hair, thin-frame glasses, warm smile" — and only that text travels through the rest of the game. The image itself is already gone.
about.differentBody2: That design makes the fun part fully explainable: you can see exactly which written traits drove each suggestion, because written traits are all the system ever has to work with.
about.neverBody1: It will never tell you who you are, claim you look identical to a real person, or guess anything sensitive — ethnicity, religion, health, personality, attractiveness, and income are permanently out of bounds, and those limits are enforced in code, not just in policy.
about.neverBody2: It will also never store, share, or train on your photo. There are no accounts and no galleries; every round starts clean and ends clean.
about.moneyBody1: The game is free to play. To keep it running we may show a small number of clearly separated ads on content pages like this one — never inside the game and never next to your results — and we accept entirely optional support payments.
about.moneyBody2: If a paid option is ever offered, its exact price is shown clearly before you pay, and paying never changes what happens to your photo: nothing is ever stored either way.
about.technologyBody1: Twinzy runs on a small web stack with a vision-capable AI model (Google's Gemini API) used for exactly one step: reading the visible traits of your photo into text. Matching, scoring, and explanation all happen over that text afterwards.
about.technologyBody2: Every AI response is validated against a strict schema and a safety filter before it reaches your screen, so a malformed or unsafe answer is dropped rather than shown.
about.contactBody1: The FAQ answers the most common questions about privacy, accuracy, and how rounds work, and the help page covers quick troubleshooting for failed rounds and unclear photos.
about.contactBody2: Spotted something wrong, unclear, or unsafe? We genuinely want to know — the project improves fastest when players tell us exactly what confused or concerned them.

howItWorks.metaDescription: Every step of a Twinzy round, in order: explicit consent, one in-memory read of your photo into written traits, immediate deletion, text-only matching, and scored, explained results.
howItWorks.title: How Twinzy works
howItWorks.intro: A complete round takes under a minute. Here is every step in order, including what happens to your photo and what the AI is allowed to do at each stage.
howItWorks.consentBody1: You pick a photo from your device or take one with your camera, and you tick an explicit consent box that describes exactly what will happen: visible traits will be read from the photo, and everything afterwards works on text only.
howItWorks.consentBody2: Nothing is uploaded until you press analyze. Closing the tab or cancelling at any point aborts the round and clears the photo.
howItWorks.extractionBody1: Our server sends the photo to the AI exactly once and asks a narrow question: what is visibly there? The answer comes back as short written traits — hair color and texture, glasses, facial hair, face shape, expression, apparent style — plus notes about anything unclear.
howItWorks.extractionBody2: The AI is instructed not to guess who you are and not to infer anything sensitive; it describes only what a stranger could see at a glance.
howItWorks.wipeBody1: Immediately after the traits are extracted, the photo is deleted from server memory — on success, on failure, and on cancellation alike. It is never written to disk, never logged, and never cached.
howItWorks.wipeBody2: From this point the game holds only a text description. Nothing remains that could identify your face.
howItWorks.matchingBody1: The written traits are compared with publicly known style descriptions of public figures. Because this stage only ever receives text, it is structurally impossible for it to compare your face with anyone's face.
howItWorks.matchingBody2: Candidates are suggested for overall style and vibe similarity — hair, glasses, expression, general aesthetic — not for facial geometry.
howItWorks.scoringBody1: Each candidate is scored for how much of the written description overlaps, then the top matches come back with a rank, a percentage-style score, and a readable reason: which traits matched strongly, which were weak, and which clearly conflicted.
howItWorks.scoringBody2: If you play in Arabic, the finished result is translated as text so the explanation stays natural in your language — the photo plays no part in translation.
howItWorks.seeNeverTitle: What the AI sees — and never sees
howItWorks.seeNeverBody1: Sees once: the single photo you chose, in memory. Sees always: written visible traits. Never sees: your name, your location, your contacts, any other photos, or any identity information.
howItWorks.seeNeverBody2: Never produces: identifications, exact-lookalike claims, or sensitive judgments. Those are blocked by validation that runs on every AI answer, every round.

aiSafety.metaDescription: The safety rules Twinzy enforces in code on every round: no identification, no sensitive inference, consent before processing, validated and filtered AI output, and honest limitations.
aiSafety.title: AI safety at Twinzy
aiSafety.intro: A game that points an AI at a personal photo has to be strict about what that AI may and may not do. These are the rules Twinzy enforces on every round — in code, not just in policy.
aiSafety.noIdentityBody1: Twinzy never determines or guesses who is in a photo. Matches are framed as playful style similarity to public figures, and wording that asserts identity — "this is", "you are", "exact lookalike" — is rejected before anything is displayed.
aiSafety.noIdentityBody2: This is also why Twinzy cannot become a face search: the matching stage never receives the photo at all, only written traits, so there is no face to search with.
aiSafety.noSensitiveBody1: The AI is forbidden from inferring ethnicity, religion, health, sexuality, personality, attractiveness, or income. Plain visible descriptors such as skin tone or hair texture may be described; conclusions about who or what a person is may not.
aiSafety.noSensitiveBody2: Any answer that crosses that line is discarded, and the round either retries safely or fails openly — unsafe content is never quietly shown.
aiSafety.consentFirstBody1: The photo is processed only after you tick a consent box whose wording describes the actual pipeline — visible-trait extraction followed by text-only matching. If the copy and the code ever disagreed, we would treat that as a bug of the highest severity.
aiSafety.consentFirstBody2: Consent applies to a single round. There is no standing permission, because nothing is retained between rounds.
aiSafety.validationTitle: Every answer is checked before you see it
aiSafety.validationBody1: AI output is machine-validated against a strict schema — structure, fields, score ranges — and a safety filter that rejects forbidden wording, sensitive topics, and clinical biometric phrasing, before it is shown or shared.
aiSafety.validationBody2: A response that fails either check is dropped. This runs at every stage — trait extraction, matching, translation — not just on the final answer.
aiSafety.limitationsBody1: The model reads one photo, once, and photos vary — so results vary. Lighting, angle, resolution, and filters all shift the written traits and therefore the matches. A different photo of you is genuinely a different round.
aiSafety.limitationsBody2: Public-figure style descriptions are also imperfect and change over time. Scores are best read as "how much of the written description overlapped", not as any deeper truth about you.
aiSafety.feedbackBody1: If a result ever seems to identify someone, make a sensitive claim, or otherwise break these rules, treat it as a bug — that is exactly what the validation layer exists to prevent.
aiSafety.feedbackBody2: Stop using that result and tell us through the routes described on the about page; safety reports get priority over everything else.

faq.metaDescription: Answers to the most common questions about Twinzy: face recognition, photo storage, accuracy, cost, accounts, the AI used, sharing, failed rounds, and child safety.
faq.title: Frequently asked questions
faq.intro: Short, honest answers to the questions players ask most.
faq.a1: No. Face recognition compares biometric measurements of faces. Twinzy converts your photo into written style traits, deletes the photo, and matches only over that text. No face templates or embeddings are ever created.
faq.a2: No. It is processed in server memory for the seconds needed to read visible traits, then wiped — on success, failure, and cancellation alike. It is never saved, logged, cached, or used for training.
faq.q3: Will Twinzy tell me who I look like exactly?
faq.a3: No. It suggests public figures with a similar overall style and vibe based on written traits, with a score and reasons. It never claims identity or an exact resemblance — that is a deliberate safety rule, not a missing feature.
faq.q4: Why do I get different results from different photos?
faq.a4: Because the AI can only describe what it sees in that specific photo. Lighting, angle, expression, and image quality change the written traits, and the traits are all the matcher ever receives.
faq.a5: Playing is free. We may show a small number of ads on content pages — never in the game or next to results — and accept optional support. If a paid option is offered, its exact price is shown clearly before any payment.
faq.a6: No. There are no accounts, profiles, or sign-ins. Every round is independent and leaves nothing behind.
faq.a7: The server uses Google's Gemini API for one narrow task: describing the visible traits of your photo as text. Matching, scoring, and explanations then run over that text, and every response is validated and safety-filtered before display.
faq.a8: Yes — sharing creates a temporary, text-only page with your matches that expires automatically. It never contains your photo, which no longer exists by that point.
faq.q9: Why did my round fail or return no match?
faq.a9: Usually the photo was too unclear for confident traits — low light, an extreme angle, heavy filters — or the traits were too generic. The game says so honestly instead of inventing a match; a clearer photo usually fixes it.
faq.a10: The game is designed to be safe and non-judgmental, but it is meant for adults and for teens with a parent's guidance. A parent or guardian should give the consent for any child's photo — and remember: nothing is stored either way.

privacy.title: Privacy
privacy.metaDescription: How Twinzy handles your photo: one in-memory read into written traits, immediate deletion, no accounts, no biometric data, plus full disclosure of the third-party services and ads that keep the game free.
privacy.intro: Twinzy is built to know as little about you as possible.
privacy.photoNotStored: Your photo is never stored. It is processed only to read visible traits, then destroyed.
privacy.traitsOnly: After visible traits are extracted, matching works only with written descriptions like hair color or face shape.
privacy.noFaceRecognition: There is no face recognition, identity matching, or biometric comparison of any kind.
privacy.noTemplates: We never create or keep face templates, embeddings, or any biometric data.
privacy.geminiNote: Our server uses the Google Gemini API to read visible traits and suggest playful matches.
privacy.funOnly: Results are for fun only and can be wrong.
privacy.freeNote: Playing is free. If an optional paid feature is offered, its price is shown clearly first, and card details go to the payment provider — never to us.
privacy.lifecycleTitle: The life of a photo, in full
privacy.lifecycleBody1: Your photo travels encrypted to our server only after you consent, is held in memory just long enough for visible traits to be written down, and is then wiped — whether the round succeeded, failed, or was cancelled. It is never written to disk, logged, backed up, or used for training.
privacy.lifecycleBody2: Everything after that moment — matching, scoring, translation, sharing — receives only the written traits. There is no copy of the image left to leak, sell, or hand over.
privacy.notCollectedBody1: No accounts, no names, no email addresses, no contact lists, no galleries, no location. We do not build profiles and have no user database.
privacy.notCollectedBody2: Shared result pages are temporary, text-only, and expire on their own; they never include the photo.
privacy.thirdPartiesBody1: Trait extraction uses Google's Gemini API: the photo is sent for that single read under the API's data-usage terms and is not used to train Google's models. Optional payments are processed by PayPal or Paymob — card details go to the payment provider, never to us.
privacy.thirdPartiesBody2: We share no photo, no traits, and no results with advertisers. Ad systems (described below) run only on content pages and are completely separate from the game pipeline.
privacy.advertisingBody1: To keep Twinzy free we may show ads from Google AdSense on content pages such as this one — never inside the game and never beside your results. Google and its partners may use cookies to serve and measure ads, including personalized ads where the law allows it.
privacy.advertisingBody2: You can opt out of personalized advertising at adssettings.google.com and read how Google uses ad data at policies.google.com/technologies/ads. The game itself works identically with ads blocked.
privacy.choicesBody1: Play without sharing: results only leave your device if you create a share link. Play without ads: content pages are the only place ads can appear, and blocking them does not affect the game.
privacy.choicesBody2: Because nothing about you is retained, there is no stored data to request or delete — closing the tab is a full erase.

terms.title: Terms
terms.noSeriousUse: Do not use results for identity checks, hiring, dating decisions, or anything serious.
terms.permission: Only upload photos of yourself, or of someone who gave you clear permission.
terms.mayBeWrong: Results are playful guesses based on written traits. They can be wrong.
terms.noHarassment: Do not use results to harass, shame, or judge anyone.

help.title: Help
help.a1: You upload one photo. We read visible traits like hair and face shape as text, destroy the photo, then suggest public figures with a similar overall style/vibe from those written traits.
help.a2: No. Matching works only on written trait descriptions, never on your actual face data.
help.a3: No. It lives in memory just long enough to read the visible traits, then it is destroyed.
help.a4: Nothing. The game is completely free.
help.q5: Why did I get no match?
help.a5: Sometimes the traits are too generic or unclear for a confident playful match. Try a clearer, well-lit photo.
